title: Astral & Zodiacal Imagery of Antiquity
creator: various
place: Egypt, the Roman Empire, and the Eastern Mediterranean
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Astronomical and astrological imagery from antiquity — the Dendera zodiac, late-antique synagogue zodiac mosaics, Mithraic tauroctony and leontocephaline cosmology, the Farnese Atlas, Egyptian tomb ceilings, and the Antikythera mechanism — recording how Egyptian, Greco-Roman, Jewish, and Persian-derived traditions mapped the heavens onto stone, mosaic, and bronze.

title: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
creator: various
date: Middle Kingdom – Roman period (c. 2000 BCE – 200 CE)
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The material culture of ancient Egyptian magic (heka): protective and funerary amulets — wedjat eyes, scarabs, djed pillars, tyet knots — alongside Book of the Dead vignettes, Horus cippi against venom, apotropaic ivory wands, and the figures (Bes, Taweret, Harpocrates) who guarded the living and the dead.

title: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
creator: various
place: Roman Egypt and the Mediterranean world
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The material remains of ritual magic in the Greco-Roman and Roman-Egyptian world: spell books on papyrus (the PGM), lead curse tablets, pierced binding figurines, inscribed amulets and engraved magical gems bearing deities like Abrasax and Chnoubis.

title: Mesopotamian Incantation Bowls & Apotropaic Demonology
creator: various
place: Mesopotamia (Babylonia, Assyria) and the Sasanian Near East
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Late-antique Aramaic, Mandaic and Syriac magic bowls inscribed with spiralling demon-binding spells, together with the older Mesopotamian apotropaic objects — Lamashtu plaques, Pazuzu amulets, protective genii and demon masks — from which their exorcistic logic descends.

title: Ancient Magical & Gnostic Gems
creator: various
place: Roman Egypt and the Eastern Mediterranean
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Engraved hardstone amulets of the Roman imperial period (1st–4th c. CE), carved with deities, voces magicae, and protective figures such as Abraxas, Chnoubis, and Harpocrates, used as personal talismans for healing and protection.

title: Medieval Astrological & Astronomical Imagery
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Astrological and astronomical illustration of the Latin Middle Ages and its early-printed afterlife (9th–16th c.): the anatomical Zodiac Man, the Children of the Planets, calendar zodiacs and labours of the months, the sphere of Sacrobosco, constellation cycles after Aratus, and the Arabic-Latin learning of Albumasar, joining medical and cosmological imagery to the doctrine that the heavens govern the body and the year.

title: Medieval & Early Grimoire Imagery
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Diagrams, seals, and figures from the European learned-magic tradition of grimoires and ritual handbooks — Solomonic pentacles, conjuration circles, planetary talismans, the Sigillum Dei, Ars Notoria notae, Picatrix astral magic, and magic squares — spanning the medieval manuscript culture and its early-modern printed afterlife.

title: Greco-Roman Mystery Cults & Esoteric Iconography
creator: various
place: Roman Empire and the Eastern Mediterranean
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The initiatory mystery religions of the Greco-Roman world — Mithraism, the Eleusinian and Dionysiac mysteries, the cults of Isis and Serapis, Cybele/Magna Mater, Sabazios, and Orphism — and their secret iconography of bull-slaying, rebirth, lion-headed Time, and the descent and return of the soul.

title: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
creator: various
place: Italy, England, France, Spain, the Low Countries
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: How Renaissance, Baroque and 19th-century artists re-imagined the magicians, witches and prophets of classical and biblical antiquity — Circe and Medea, the Witch of Endor raising Samuel, the sibyls who foretold Christ, Lucan's necromancer Erichtho, Saturn the devouring melancholic, and the Isis-initiation novel of Apuleius. The works are dated to the later artwork, not the lost ancient original.

title: Esoteric Cosmological & Microcosm–Macrocosm Diagrams
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Cosmological and microcosm–macrocosm diagrams of the Latin Middle Ages and early modern period (7th–17th c.): Isidore's T-O and rotae, Byrhtferth's computistic wheel, Hildegard's cosmic man, the Liber Floridus and Hortus Deliciarum, Ramon Llull's combinatorial wheels and tree of knowledge, the Great Chain of Being, Apian's and Cellarius's geocentric spheres, and Fludd's macrocosm — visual arguments that the structure of the cosmos, the elements, and the human being mirror one another.

title: Demonology, the Goetia & the Iconography of Devils
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A gallery of devils and demons from the European grimoire and demonological imagination — Louis Le Breton's engravings for Collin de Plancy's Dictionnaire Infernal, the seals of the Lemegeton's Ars Goetia, the Faust pact, the Temptation of St Anthony, hell-mouth and witches'-sabbath scenes — spanning medieval manuscript devils to the encyclopedic demon portraits of the 19th century.

title: Kabbalah & Jewish Magic
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Diagrams, books, amulets, and reception images from the Jewish mystical and magical tradition and its Christian-Kabbalah afterlife — Sefirotic Trees of Life (Gikatilla, Ricius, Fludd, Knorr von Rosenroth), the divine-names magic of the Sefer Raziel, shiviti and mizrah devotional plates, the High Priest's breastplate, the Merkabah of Ezekiel's chariot, and the Golem and Lilith of legend, spanning the medieval Kabbalah through its early-modern print culture.

title: Renaissance Learned Magic & Its Diagrams
creator: various
place: Western Europe (Italy, German lands, France, England)
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Diagrams, portraits, and figures from the Renaissance revival of learned natural and ceremonial magic — Agrippa's microcosmic pentagram and angelic alphabets, John Dee's Monas and Enochian/Sigillum material, Giordano Bruno's mnemonic art, the Hermetic philosophy of Ficino, Paracelsian and Khunrath alchemy, della Porta's physiognomy of signatures, and the cosmic harmonics that linked all things by correspondence.

title: Sacred Architecture & the Esoteric Built Environment
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Photographs of carved and built sacred space in which Western architecture carries an esoteric charge: the pavement labyrinths of the Gothic cathedrals, foliate Green Men and Sheela-na-gigs, Cosmati sacred-geometry floors, cosmological rose windows, zodiac portals, apotropaic gargoyles, mandorla tympana, the Externsteine, and Renaissance garden grottoes.

title: Early-Modern Celestial Cartography & Astrology
creator: various
place: German lands, Low Countries, Danzig, Augsburg, Nuremberg
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Engraved celestial cartography and astrological imagery of the European early-modern period (1515–1690): Cellarius's baroque planispheres of the Ptolemaic, Copernican, and Tychonic world-systems and his Christianised sky; the figured constellation atlases of Bayer, Bartsch, and Hevelius; Dürer's 1515 star map; and Apianus's instrument-like astrological volvelles — the visual culture in which a learned astronomy and a practising astrology still shared one heaven.

title: Magic & the Supernatural in the Bible
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Scenes of magic and the supernatural drawn from scripture and painted across art history — Simon Magus falling, Moses and Aaron's rods against Pharaoh's sorcerers, the brazen serpent, the Witch of Endor raising Samuel, Elymas struck blind, Belshazzar's writing on the wall, the Magi as astrologers, and the angelic dreams of Jacob and Christ's Transfiguration. The occult within the canonical image-world, read as a contest between licit miracle and forbidden art.

title: The Arts of Divination
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A survey of the divinatory arts from antiquity to the nineteenth century — Etruscan and Roman haruspicy and augury, geomancy and chiromancy, della Porta and Lavater physiognomy, Cardano-school metoposcopy, John Dee's scrying mirror, the historic (pre-Waite) tarot of the Mantegna Tarocchi, the Visconti courts and the Tarot de Marseille, and the fortune-teller genre of Caravaggio and Georges de La Tour.

title: Magical & Esoteric Objects and Ritual Instruments
creator: various
place: Europe, the Near East, and beyond
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Three-dimensional instruments and apparatus of the Western and Near Eastern magical traditions — John Dee's scrying tools, astrolabes and armillary spheres, talismanic rings and seals, witch-bottles and concealed shoes, incantation bowls, alchemical glassware, and amuletic jewellery — photographed as surviving objects in museum collections.

title: Rosicrucian & Hermetic Emblematic Engravings (1600–1800)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / No-restrictions (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Engravings and coloured plates of the Rosicrucian and Hermetic current in the German lands and across Europe, 1614–1785: the manifesto literature (Fama Fraternitatis), Theophilus Schweighardt's winged Temple of the Rosy Cross, Robert Fludd's Utriusque Cosmi engravings, Michael Maier's emblem books (Atalanta Fugiens, Symbola Aureae Mensae), Heinrich Khunrath's Amphitheatrum citadel and oratory-laboratory, the wordless Mutus Liber, Daniel Cramer's heart-emblems, Jacob Böhme's theosophical globe, Comenius's emblematic primer, and the famous coloured plates of the Geheime Figuren der Rosenkreuzer (Secret Symbols of the Rosicrucians).

title: The Visual Culture of Witchcraft, 1450–1820
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The image-world of the European witch from the first illustrated demonologies to Goya — the broom-riding heretic of Le Franc's Champion des Dames, Ulrich Molitor's storm-cooking woodcuts, Baldung Grien's and Dürer's eroticized German prints, the kitchen and sabbath scenes of Francken, Teniers, Rosa and Ziarnko, the English witch-finder's familiars, and Goya's Caprichos satires of flight and credulity.

title: Eastern Esoterica: Magic & Inner Alchemy of Asia
creator: various
place: Asia (China, Tibet, Japan, India, Thailand, Mongolia)
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A survey of the magical and inner-alchemical traditions of Asia: Taoist internal-alchemy body charts (the Neijing Tu and Xiuzhen Tu) and paper fú talismans, the Taijitu and bagua trigrams, the I Ching hexagram diagrams Leibniz studied, the Luoshu magic square and the feng-shui luopan, Tibetan Vajrayana mandalas (Kalachakra, Hevajra) and wrathful protectors, the Bardo Thodol deities, the Japanese Diamond and Womb mandalas, Abe no Seimei's pentagram and Shinto ofuda/omamori, the Hindu Sri Yantra and chakra subtle-body diagrams, Thai sak-yant, and Mongolian shamanic instruments.

title: Freemasonry & Fraternal Esoteric Symbolism (18th–19th c.)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Prints, regalia, and lodge furnishings of speculative Freemasonry and the allied fraternal/Rosicrucian societies, c. 1750–1920: the three degree tracing boards (Bowring's Emulation set and their kin), the Square and Compasses, the All-Seeing Eye of Providence, the Master Mason and Hiram Abiff legend, the pillars Jachin and Boaz, the 47th Problem of Euclid, the Masonic apron and officers' jewels, engraved certificates and emblematic charts, the lodge interior with its mosaic pavement, and the great anti-Masonic exposures of William Morgan and Léo Taxil. An esoteric idiom that absorbed Hermetic, Kabbalistic, and Rosicrucian symbolism into the ritual stage-craft of the lodge.

title: Gnostic & Hermetic Primary Sources (Codices, Manichaean & Mandaean Art, Hermetica)
creator: various
place: Egypt, Eastern Mediterranean, Central Asia & Europe
language: Coptic, Greek, Latin, Middle Persian & Mandaic
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Surviving primary-source images of the Gnostic and Hermetic textual traditions: the Coptic Nag Hammadi codices and the Jung Codex, Manichaean silk paintings (the Diagram of the Universe and Bema sermons from the Chinese and Turfan corpus), Mandaean baptismal rite, depictions of Hermes Trismegistus from the Siena Cathedral pavement to printed Hermetica, Marsilio Ficino's Latin Corpus Hermeticum, the Emerald Tablet in Khunrath's Amphitheatrum, and a Holy Wisdom (Sophia) icon.

title: Islamicate Occult Sciences
creator: various
place: Near East, Persia, Egypt, Ottoman and Mughal lands
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The occult sciences of the Islamicate world (10th–18th c.) — the chief conduit by which Greek and Indian astrology, alchemy and talismanic magic reached the Latin West. It gathers the demon-kings and zodiac figures of the Kitāb al-Bulhān, the angels and jinn of al-Qazwīnī's Wonders of Creation, al-Ṣūfī's constellation figures, the Falnāma divinatory paintings, Ottoman talismanic shirts and magic squares, brass astrolabes, celestial globes and geomantic instruments, and the alchemical-magical recipe books standing behind the Picatrix and Latin alchemy.

title: Norse, Germanic & Northern-European Magic
creator: various
place: Scandinavia, Iceland, England, and the Germanic North
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The magical material culture of the Germanic and Northern-European world — Icelandic magical staves (galdrastafir) such as the Ægishjálmur and Vegvísir, runestones and curse-stones, runic amulets and gold bracteates, Thor's-hammer pendants, Odin picture-stones, the valknut and triquetra, and the Sámi shaman's drum — surviving in manuscripts, on carved stone, and as museum objects.

title: Spiritualism & the Séance: The Occult Revival's Visual Record
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The visual record of the modern occult revival, from the Hydesville rappings of the Fox sisters (1848) to the Cottingley fairies (1917–20): spirit photographs by Mumler and Hope, ectoplasm plates from Schrenck-Notzing's séances with Eva Carrière, the Davenport spirit cabinet and Eusapia Palladino's levitations, the Ouija board and the planchette, Theosophical thought-forms and aura plates by Besant and Leadbeater, the mediumistic art of Georgiana Houghton, and the pseudosciences of mesmerism and phrenology that fed the movement.

title: Visionary Alchemical Emblem-Series (1550–1785)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The great visionary emblem-series of early-modern alchemy beyond the canonical Atalanta Fugiens and Splendor Solis: the famous squared-circle Azoth of the philosophers attributed to Basilius Valentinus, the Twelve Keys with their kings, dragons and birds, the union-flask sequence of the Donum Dei, the green lion devouring the sun, the Rebis hermaphrodite standing on the dragon, the Ouroboros of Cleopatra, Hermes' Tabula Smaragdina, and the engraved emblem-books of Michael Maier, Johann Daniel Mylius and Daniel Stolcius — the iconographic backbone of the spagyric Great Work.

title: The Apocalypse: Visionary & Occult Imagery of Revelation
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The supernatural imagination of the Book of Revelation across Western art — Albrecht Dürer's epoch-making Apocalypse woodcuts (the Four Horsemen, St Michael and the Dragon, the Whore of Babylon), the Mozarabic Beatus of Liébana commentaries of medieval Spain, the Angers Apocalypse Tapestry, the Carolingian Trier and Ottonian Bamberg Apocalypses, the Cloisters Apocalypse, William Blake's visionary watercolours, and Last Judgement imagery from Giotto, Memling, and Signorelli's Antichrist — a gallery of cosmic monsters, hellmouths, and the seer of Patmos.

title: William Blake — Visionary & Prophetic Art
creator: William Blake
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The supreme esoteric-visionary art of William Blake (1757–1827): the relief-etched illuminated prophecies (Urizen, Europe, The Marriage of Heaven and Hell, Jerusalem, Milton, Songs of Innocence and of Experience) and the large colour-printed designs and tempera/watercolour series (Newton, The Ancient of Days, the Great Red Dragon, Nebuchadnezzar, The Ghost of a Flea, Pity, Hecate, Elohim Creating Adam, and the illustrations to the Book of Job and Dante). Blake fused Bible, Milton, Swedenborgian and Behmenist mysticism, and his own private mythology into a self-built visionary cosmos.

title: Memento Mori: The Art of Death, the Danse Macabre & the Triumph of Death
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The medieval and early-modern imagery of death and the great leveller: the Danse Macabre of Paris, Lübeck, Basel and Holbein; the Triumph of Death from Palermo and Pisa to Bruegel; the deathbed contests of the Ars moriendi; transi tombs and vanitas still-lifes; the Three Living and Three Dead; the Wheel of Fortune; plague, pestilence and the Apocalyptic horseman — the visual culture in which the alchemical nigredo and the apotropaic skull are rooted.

title: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
creator: various
place: Europe and the wider Mediterranean / Atlantic world
rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The everyday occult of cunning-folk and householders — the apotropaic marks scratched on barns and church timbers (the daisy-wheel hexafoil, taper burn-marks), Pennsylvania-Dutch hex signs, the evil-eye amulets of the Mediterranean (nazar, cornicello, mano cornuta, fascinum, the cimaruta), horseshoes and witch-balls hung against harm, poppets and hag-stones, the masks of Krampus and the Alpine Perchten, the SATOR square and abracadabra charms, Ethiopian magic scrolls, and the printed pow-wow charm-books of the Pennsylvania Germans.

title: The Nineteenth-Century Occult Revival
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item; one modern CC BY-SA redrawing). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The visual vocabulary of the nineteenth-century occult revival and the historic decks it canonised — Éliphas Lévi's pentagram and the engravings of his Transcendental Magic, Oswald Wirth's 1889 esoteric Tarot (the Magician, the Devil, the Wheel, the Star/Moon plate), Stanislas de Guaita's goat-pentagram and the illustrations to La Clef de la Magie Noire, the fifteenth-century Sola Busca and the Mantegna and Minchiate tarots that fed the revival, Kircher's kabbalistic Tree of Life, John Dee's Sigillum Dei Aemeth as inherited by the Golden Dawn, the Rose Cross lamen, and the seal of the Theosophical Society.

title: Christian Cabala & Pythagorean Number-Mysticism
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The Renaissance fusion of Jewish Kabbalah, Pythagorean number-mysticism, and Neoplatonic cosmology into a Christian esoteric philosophy — Pico della Mirandola's and Reuchlin's cabalist treatises, the Pentagrammaton and Tetragrammaton name-magic, the Pythagorean tetractys and decad, Llull's combinatorial art, Francesco Giorgio's musical-harmonic cosmos, the Neoplatonic emanation of Proclus, and the theosophy of Boehme, Fludd, Kircher, and Postel.

title: Demons & Spirits of the Manuscript Grimoires
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Demons and spirits as illustrated in the manuscript grimoire tradition rather than the printed Dictionnaire Infernal — the coloured grotesques of the Compendium rarissimum, the spirits of the Grand Grimoire and Black Pullet, Goetic spirit-seals, conjured apparitions, fallen angels and apocalyptic beasts, and the demon-binding scenes of the Solomonic literature, spanning medieval illumination to the nineteenth-century occult revival.

title: Charaktêres & Magical Sign-Systems
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The ring-letter signs (charaktêres) of the Greek Magical Papyri and their long descendants: the looped 'spectacle-letter' signs on PGM papyri, curse tablets and engraved gems of the Greco-Roman world; the magical alphabets of the Renaissance grimoires (Theban, Celestial, Malachim, Transitus Fluvii); the magic-square-derived planetary seals; and the character-diagrams of John Dee. A single tradition of writing meant not to be read but to compel.

title: Magicians at Work: Conjurers, Necromancers & the Magic Circle
creator: various
place: Western Europe (England, France, German lands, Italy, Near East)
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Prints and paintings of ceremonial magicians, necromancers, and conjurers performing their operations — the magus in his study, the necromancer raising the dead, Faust in the circle facing Mephistopheles, John Dee at Elizabeth's court and Edward Kelley over an open grave, Solomon commanding demons, the Witch of Endor calling up Samuel, and the diviner casting his figures. The visual archive of magic as something done.

title: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Printed editions, manuscript pages, and diagrams of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation) and the wider tradition of Hebrew letter-combination magic — the Mantua 1562 editio princeps title page and text, the thirty-two paths of wisdom (ten sefirot + twenty-two letters), the three mothers / seven doubles / twelve simples, alphabet-and-zodiac charts, gematria and number-mysticism plates from Kircher, the Tetragrammaton and its vowel-points, and Renaissance Christian-Kabbalah witnesses such as Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*.

title: The Solomonic Grimoire Tradition
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Circles, pentacles, seals, and ritual apparatus of the Solomonic cycle — the Goetia circle and Triangle of Art of the Lemegeton, the seven planetary Pentacles of Solomon from Mathers's 1888 Key of Solomon, the magical circles and seals of 19th-century Clavicula manuscripts, the Cyprianus and Clavis Inferni 'black book' seals, and the black-evocation circle of the Grand Grimoire — spanning the medieval Latin tradition and its early-modern and revival afterlife.

title: The Kabbalistic Tree of Life
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Images of the Kabbalistic Tree of Life (*Etz Chaim*) — the diagram of the ten *sefirot* and twenty-two connective paths through which the Infinite (*Ein Sof*) emanates the worlds. The set spans medieval and Renaissance Hebrew sources (Gikatilla's *Sha'arei Orah* / *Portae Lucis*, the *Sefer Yetzirah*), Lurianic diagrams from the school of Isaac Luria, the Christian-Hebraist trees of the Sulzbach and Württemberg circles (the Bad Teinach *Lehrtafel*), the four worlds and the cosmic-man *Adam Kadmon*, and modern scholarly reconstructions of the three-pillar tree with its divine names and Hebrew letters. Deliberately seeks editions and plates distinct from those already held under jewish_mysticism.

title: Planetary Seals & Magical Sigils
creator: after H. C. Agrippa / Francis Barrett
rights: Public domain (vector renderings; one CC BY-SA). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The drawn signs of magic — the planetary seals derived from the kameas of Agrippa and Barrett, the Pentagrammaton, and the goetic sigils — the abstract characters through which spirits were named and bound.

title: Seals of the 72 Spirits (Ars Goetia)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 / one CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The individual seals of the seventy-two demons of the Ars Goetia, the first book of the Lesser Key of Solomon — each spirit's proper signature, by which the conjurer named, summoned and bound it, with its rank, office and legions per the Mathers/Crowley edition of 1904.

title: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The deeper figural machinery of astrology beyond the twelve signs: the Children of the Planets cycles in which each planet rules a temperament and a class of trades; the planetary deities riding their chariots; the 36 decans and the 28 mansions of the Moon; and the great fresco programmes of Renaissance Italy — the Salone dei Mesi at Palazzo Schifanoia in Ferrara, the astrological hall of the Salone in Padua — that fused decan figures, planet-rulers, and the labours of the months into a single image of cosmic government.

title: The Emblem Tradition & the Theory of the Hidden Image
creator: various
place: Italy, German lands, the Low Countries, England, France
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The symbolic-emblem tradition and the theory of the hidden image: from Alciato's founding Emblematum liber (1531) through Ripa's Iconologia of personified abstractions, the Renaissance 'hieroglyphics' of Horapollo and Valeriano, the Hermetic emblems of Bocchi, the love- and moral-emblems of Vaenius and Quarles, and the dense allegorical title pages of occult-philosophical books by Fludd, Kircher and Khunrath. These works built the visual grammar in which an abstract or esoteric idea could be encoded in a picture and read like a sacred sign.

title: Egyptosophy — The Hermetic & Baroque Reception of Egyptian Wisdom
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Images of 'Egyptosophy' — the Renaissance and Baroque conviction that Egyptian hieroglyphs and gods encoded a primordial Hermetic theology. Spanning the Bembine Tablet (Mensa Isiaca) at Turin, Athanasius Kircher's Egyptological folios (Oedipus Aegyptiacus, Obeliscus Pamphilius, Sphinx Mystagoga, Mundus Subterraneus), the hieroglyph compendia of Horapollo and Pierio Valeriano, the Hypnerotomachia's dream-obelisk, the re-erected obelisks of Baroque Rome, and the gods Apis, Thoth, and Isis as the Western esoteric tradition imagined them.

title: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
creator: various
date: Late Postclassic – early colonial (c. 1300–1600 CE); some objects earlier
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The screenfold divinatory books of Postclassic Mesoamerica — the Codex Borgia group (Borgia, Vaticanus B, Cospi, Laud, Fejérváry-Mayer), the Aztec Borbonicus and Codex Ríos, and the Maya Dresden and Madrid codices — together with the gods who govern their 260-day count: Quetzalcoatl, Tezcatlipoca, the death-lord Mictlantecuhtli, the filth-eater Tlazolteotl, and the star-demon tzitzimime. These almanacs map fate, time, the four directions, and the calendar onto a ritual cosmos, crowned by the Aztec Sun Stone.

title: Sacred Geometry & Number-Mysticism
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Renaissance and early-modern prints and diagrams in which geometry carries cosmic and esoteric meaning: Leonardo's and Agrippa's microcosmic men in circle and square, the Platonic solids of Pacioli and Kepler, the divine proportion and golden spiral, the vesica piscis, the squaring of the circle, monochord harmonics, Dürer's constructions, and Robert Fludd's geometric cosmos.

title: Historic Tarot: Trumps Before the Occult Revival
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Tarot in its first three centuries, before the late-eighteenth-century occultists reinvented it as a hermetic book. The set gathers the hand-painted gold-ground trumps of the Visconti-Sforza deck (Milan, c. 1450), the engraved didactic 'Mantegna Tarocchi' of Ferrara (c. 1465), the woodcut Tarot de Marseille in the patterns of Jean Dodal (Lyon, c. 1701) and Nicolas Conver (Marseille, 1760), tracing the iconography of the World, the Star, the Sun, the Moon, Death, the Devil, Justice and their fellows from courtly luxury object to mass-printed playing pack.

title: The Esoteric Bestiary: Alchemical Animals & Monstrous Wonders
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A bestiary of occult and alchemical creatures from the European imagination — the green lion and the dragon of prima materia, the ouroboros and the rebis, the phoenix and salamander of fire, alongside the wonder-beasts of the great early-modern natural histories of Aldrovandi, Gesner, Olaus Magnus, Paré and Kircher: basilisk and cockatrice, griffin, harpy and siren, unicorn, sea monsters and Leviathan, the mandrake and the homunculus.

title: Reading the Body: Physiognomy, Chiromancy & the Divinatory Arts of the Flesh
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The divinatory arts that read fate and character from the surface of the body: della Porta's human-animal physiognomy and Lavater's silhouettes and profiles, the palmistry hands with their planetary mounts and lines, Cardano-school metoposcopy of the forehead, Gall and Spurzheim's phrenological heads and skulls, the zodiac-man of medical astrology, and the lesser arts of onychomancy (nails) and iris-reading.

title: Portents & Wonders: Celestial Signs, Prodigies & the Wonder-Books
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The occult reading of signs in the heavens and on earth across the early-modern wonder-books and broadsheets: the Augsburg Book of Miracles (*Wunderzeichenbuch*, c. 1550) with its full-page paintings of comets, three suns, mock-moons, mondhalos, dragons over Bohemia, fiery sky-battles and the Tiber monster; the 1561 Nuremberg 'battle in the sky'; the 1726 aurora; Lycosthenes' *Prodigiorum chronicon*; Ambroise Paré's monsters; the Cranach Pope-Ass and Monk-Calf of Wittenberg; the Bayeux comet (Halley); and the cometary atlases of Hevelius and Lubieniecki — a gallery of celestial portents read as divine warnings.

title: The Renaissance Hieroglyph & Mythography
creator: various
place: Venice, Bologna, Rome, Lyon, Augsburg, Basel
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The Renaissance belief — sparked by the 1419 rediscovery of Horapollo's *Hieroglyphica* — that the Egyptian hieroglyphs were a picture-language encoding hidden divine wisdom, and that pagan myth was a veiled theology. The collection gathers the woodcut frieze of the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (1499) with its anchor-and-dolphin *festina lente*, the Aldine printer's device, the encyclopaedic hieroglyph-books of Pierio Valeriano, Horapollo's printed editions, Achille Bocchi's *Symbolicae Quaestiones* (the Hermathena), and the illustrated mythographies of Vincenzo Cartari, in which the gods of antiquity become legible allegories.

title: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia — Dictionary of Personifications
creator: Cesare Ripa (and later illustrators)
place: Rome / Italy (with German and English editions)
language: Italian (with Latin titles; English and German editions)
rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Cesare Ripa's Iconologia (first published 1593, first illustrated 1603) is the canonical early-modern dictionary of allegorical personifications: it taught painters, sculptors, and frontispiece-designers how to give an abstract idea a recognizable body by assigning it fixed attributes. This collection gathers individual concept-plates from several editions (1603, 1611, 1613, the 1644 English 'Tempest' edition, and the 1758-64 Hertel/Italian editions), favouring the personifications most relevant to esoteric and natural-philosophical thought — the Elements, Temperaments, Magic, Alchemy, Astrology, Wisdom, Nature, Truth, and the sciences.

title: Theosophical & Modern-Esoteric Diagrams
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item; several modern CC BY / CC BY-SA redrawings of PD designs). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The diagrammatic and clairvoyant imagery of the Theosophical movement and its German offshoot Anthroposophy, c. 1877–1925: the planetary-chain and root-race schemes of Blavatsky's Secret Doctrine, the colour-plates of clairvoyant emotion from Besant and Leadbeater's Thought-Forms (1905), the aura and subtle-body charts of Leadbeater's Man Visible and Invisible (1902), the syncretic seal of the Theosophical Society, portraits of Blavatsky, Besant, Leadbeater and Olcott, the Mahatma letters, and Rudolf Steiner's blackboard drawings.

title: African & Diaspora Divination, Sign-Systems & Power Objects
creator: various
date: c. 4000 BP rock art – early 20th century CE (objects); modern symbol renderings
place: West, Central & Southern Africa; Madagascar; Atlantic diaspora
language: Yoruba / Akan (Twi) / Kikongo / Luba / Chokwe / Fon / Malagasy / Geʿez
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A photographic survey of African and African-diaspora divination and ritual sign-systems: the Yoruba Ifá apparatus (opon Ifá tray, iroke tapper, opele chain, agere cup, the babalawo at work), Akan Adinkra ideograms and figurative goldweights, Kongo nkisi/nkondi power-figures, the Luba lukasa memory board, Chokwe sona sand-drawings, Malagasy sikidy geomancy, Fon/Vodun shrines and Legba, and Ethiopian protective scrolls.

title: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The astrological reading of the sky in early-modern print and painting: the Great Comet of 1577 (the Tübingen / Daščický broadsheet over Prague, Tycho Brahe's diagram, the Istanbul sighting), the comet-form atlases of Hevelius (*Cometographia*, *Prodromus Cometicus*) and Lubieniecki (*Theatrum Cometicum*), the 1556 comet-and-earthquake over Constantinople, the 1680 comet over Rotterdam, the Kometenbuch comet-book, solar and lunar eclipses (Apian's volvelle, the Florentine Codex, the 1566 and 1832 eclipses), the Vädersolstavlan sun-dogs of 1535 Stockholm, Sebastian Brant's Ensisheim meteorite of 1492, and Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio* — comets and eclipses read as warnings written in the heavens.

title: Christian Apotropaic & Protective Magic — Sacramentals and Holy Charms
creator: various
place: German lands, the Alps, and Catholic Europe
rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The sacramentals and holy charms at the border of Catholic devotion and folk magic — the blessed Agnus Dei wax disc, the folded Breverl amulet-packet and the swallowed Schluckbildchen, the Benedictine medal and Zachariassegen against evil, the Caravaca and Ulrich crosses, the Three Kings' Epiphany door-blessing, the weather-crosses raised against hail and storm, the IHS Christogram and the arma Christi, the Drudenfuß pentagram on the threshold, the Haussegen broadside, the plague saints Roch and Sebastian, St Christopher and the Fourteen Holy Helpers, St Antony's tau, and the secret Vehmgericht tribunal.

title: Astral Talismanic Magic: Kameas, Star-Seals & Planetary Images
creator: various
place: Near East and Western Europe (Arabic-Latin transmission)
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The images by which planetary and stellar power was drawn into objects: the seven planetary magic squares (kameas), the seals, intelligences and spirits derived from them in Agrippa and Barrett, the astral images of the Picatrix, the mansions of the Moon, and engraved talismanic amulets — the operative iconography of astral magic from the Arabic Ghayat al-Hakim through Renaissance occult philosophy to its nineteenth-century revival.

title: Historic Tarot Decks (Renaissance & Early Modern)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A cross-section of the foundational tarot decks of Italy and France—the hand-painted fifteenth-century Visconti and Estensi courts, the engraved Sola Busca and Mantegna series, and the woodcut Tarot de Marseille tradition (Dodal, Vieville, Madenié, Besançon)—completing the historic coverage of the deck-by-deck record.

title: The Voynich Manuscript & the Tradition of Occult Ciphers
creator: various
place: Central Europe, England, Italy & Central Europe
language: Undeciphered 'Voynichese', Latin, English, German & enciphered scripts
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The Voynich Manuscript (Beinecke MS 408) — an early-fifteenth-century codex written in an undeciphered script and filled with unidentified plants, zodiac wheels, bathing nymphs, a nine-rosette cosmic foldout, and jars — set within the wider tradition of occult ciphers and secret writing: Trithemius's *Steganographia* and *Polygraphia*, the Copiale and Rohonc enigmas, the Dorabella and Beale ciphers, the Masonic pigpen, the Shugborough inscription, John Dee's angelic tables, and Alberti's cipher disk.

title: The Alchemist at Work: The Laboratory Genre in Painting & Print
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Paintings and prints of the alchemist in his cluttered workshop — the human face of the Great Work. From Bruegel's ruined family and Teniers' many bearded adepts to Joseph Wright of Derby's praying alchemist amid the glowing flask, the genre weighs hope against folly, blowing the bellows over furnace and alembic in pursuit of gold and the stone.

title: John Dee, Edward Kelley & the Enochian / Angelic Magic System
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Portraits, manuscripts and printed diagrams documenting the angelic-magic system received by the English mathematician and magus John Dee (1527–1608/9) and his scryer Edward Kelley (1555–1597/8) between 1582 and 1589. The collection gathers the surviving likenesses of both men (the Ashmolean and Wellcome Dee portraits, F. Cleyn's 1658 line engraving, R. Cooper's stipple and the coloured aquatint of Kelley, Sibly's necromancy print), Dee's own hand (a 1588 letter, the gold disc from the British Museum, the imperial order for Kelley's arrest, his Mortlake house and memorial), and the foundational diagrams of Enochian magic — the Holy Table (Mensa Sancta), the Order of the Inspirati and the angelic charts — as printed in Meric Casaubon's A True & Faithful Relation (London, 1659).

title: Fortune, Fate & Time: The Personified Powers of Destiny
creator: various
place: Italy, France, German lands, the Low Countries, Spain, Egypt
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The personified powers of destiny in esoteric and reception iconography: the turning Wheel of Fortune of medieval manuscripts (Boethius, the Hortus Deliciarum, Boccaccio, Lydgate), the Renaissance Fortuna with her wheel, rudder and globe, the three Fates (Moirai / Parcae) who spin, measure and cut the thread of life, bald-behind Kairos / Occasio the seized moment of Opportunity, Saturn-Chronos and Father Time with scythe and hourglass, the staircase Ages of Man, and the Norse Norns and city-goddess Tyche. Works are dated to the surviving artwork.

title: Mesmerism, Animal Magnetism & the Invisible Fluid (1780s–1850s)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY 4.0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Prints, portraits, caricatures, and objects from the heyday of mesmerism and animal magnetism, c. 1779–1880: Franz Anton Mesmer and his magnetic tub (the baquet), the magnetizer's passes and the convulsive 'crisis', Puységur's artificial somnambulism, the satirical assault on the 'magnetic' craze and the 1784 royal commissions, Reichenbach's odic force, James Braid's hypnotism, Charcot at the Salpêtrière, and the adjacent sciences of physiognomy and phrenology — a whole vocabulary of the invisible fluid coursing through the nervous body.

title: The Occult Herbal — Magical Plants & the Doctrine of Signatures
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The magical pharmacopoeia of herbalists, witches, and physicians: the anthropomorphic mandrake of the medieval herbals and the dog that uproots it, Della Porta's and Croll's doctrine of signatures, the psychoactive Solanaceae of the witches' flying ointment (henbane, belladonna, datura, monkshood, hemlock), Homer's moly against Circe, the zoophyte legends of the barnacle-goose tree and the vegetable lamb of Tartary, the apotropaic St John's wort and the flowering Glastonbury thorn, and the deadly upas tree of the Romantic imagination.

title: St Cyprian & the Christian Magician-Saints
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: A gallery of the Christian magician-saint tradition — Cyprian of Antioch (patron of magicians) and Justina, Theophilus of Adana (the first Faust whose pact the Virgin reclaims), the temptations of St Anthony, the desert father Macarius, the Gerasene/Gadarene exorcism, the fall of Simon Magus, the harrowing of hell, and Hermes Trismegistus enthroned as a pagan prophet in a cathedral floor — spanning Byzantine fresco and Romanesque manuscript to Renaissance and Baroque print.

title: Astrological & Occult Medicine: The Body Governed by the Stars
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY 4.0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons (Wellcome Collection, NLM, BSB).
blurb: The schematic human figure of late-medieval and Renaissance medicine, mapped onto the heavens: the Zodiac Man (Homo signorum) whose every limb is ruled by a sign, the Aderlassmann / Bloodletting Man marked with the proper veins and the favourable signs for phlebotomy, the Vein Man, the weapon-pierced Wound Man (Wundenmann) of the surgical handbooks, and the uroscopy charts by which the physician read disease and prognosis from the urine-flask. Drawn largely from the woodcuts of Ketham's Fasciculus medicinae, Gersdorff's Feldtbuch der Wundartzney, and the German calendar-almanacs, with manuscript witnesses from the Wellcome Collection and the National Library of Medicine.

title: Bosch, Bruegel & the Demonic Fantastic of the Low Countries
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The supreme imagery of hell, temptation and the monstrous from Hieronymus Bosch, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and their Antwerp circle (Pieter Huys, Jan Mandijn, Frans Floris). Garden-of-Earthly-Delights torments, the Tree-Man, Dulle Griet at the mouth of hell, the Triumph of Death, the demon-filled Seven Deadly Sins engravings, and a swarm of Boschian hybrid devils — the visionary fantastic that fed centuries of European demonology.

title: Lot-Books, Fortune-Games & the Oracle Tradition
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The literature and apparatus of fortune by lot, from antiquity to the modern parlour: Sigismondo Fanti's printed lot-book Triompho di Fortuna with its spinning Rotae, the German Losbuch of Konrad Bollstatter, the medieval Wheel of Fortune, the sixteen geomantic figures of 'ilm al-raml and its Malagasy cousin sikidy, the Circle of Petosiris (the prognostic Sphere of Life and Death), astragalomantic knucklebones and the Roman dodecahedron, Sibylline prophecy and the Sibyls, the I Ching's binary hexagrams, the Lenormand fortune-cards, and the spiritualist talking board.

title: Werewolves, Vampires & the Night-Terrors of European Folklore
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The image-world of the European night: Cranach's man-eating Werwolf and the Ovidian Lycaon, the vampires of Munch and the Burne-Joneses, the wild ride of Odin's Hunt, the mare and changeling of Fuseli and Abildgaard, the Berserker of the Torslunda plate, Holbein's Death, and the churchyard ghosts of Shakespearean and folkloric reception.

title: Paracelsus & the Four Elementals (1538–1909)
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Theophrastus von Hohenheim, called Paracelsus, recast Renaissance medicine around the alchemical body and peopled the cosmos with four orders of elemental beings — gnomes of earth, undines of water, sylphs of air, salamanders of fire. This collection gathers his contested portraits, the elementals as they pass from the Paracelsian *Liber de nymphis* into Romantic illustration, and the allied iconography of spagyric medicine: Croll's *Basilica chymica*, della Porta's doctrine of signatures, Fludd's microcosm and Speculum, Agricola's mine-spirits, and the zodiac/vein man of astrological surgery.

title: Esoteric Secret Societies & Initiatic Orders (18th–19th c.)
creator: various
place: German lands, France, Italy & North America
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Portraits, emblems, regalia, and conspiracy prints of the initiatic orders that branched off from and beyond craft Freemasonry, c. 1750–1900: Weishaupt's Bavarian Illuminati and its owl of Minerva, the Gold- und Rosenkreuzer and Martinist theosophy, Cagliostro's Egyptian Rite, the Templar-revival Strict Observance, the Carbonari, the Memphis-Misraïm rite, and the anti-secret-society exposures (Barruel, Robison) that fed two centuries of conspiracy myth.

title: Byzantine, Jewish & Eastern-Christian Protective Amulets
creator: various
place: Eastern Mediterranean, the Near East and the Jewish diaspora
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Late-antique, Byzantine, Eastern-Christian and Jewish protective objects — Holy Rider and 'much-suffering eye' amulets, the hystera/womb device against gynaecological demons, Gello/Abyzou and Sisinnios charms, Hebrew childbirth amulets invoking Senoy-Sansenoy-Semangelof against Lilith, gold lamellae, hamsas, the menorah token and silver kameot — spanning the apotropaic traditions of the Greek and Hebrew worlds from antiquity to the early modern era.

title: Cosmic Machines: Astronomical Clocks & Planetary Mechanisms
creator: various
place: Greece, Italy, France, German lands, Bohemia, England, Sweden, Netherlands
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Photographs and diagrams of the great clocks and computing instruments that modelled the heavens as a turning machine: the Prague Orloj and the cathedral clocks of Strasbourg, Wells, Lyon, Besançon, Münster, Lund, Venice and Olomouc; the Antikythera mechanism; the torquetum, nocturnal and astronomical compendium; the Eisinga planetarium; and the volvelles of Apian's Astronomicum Caesareum.

title: The Music of the Spheres & Cosmic Harmony
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Woodcuts, engravings and diagrams of the Pythagorean-Platonic doctrine that the cosmos is a tuned instrument: Gaffurio's Apollo-and-the-planetary-modes frontispiece and his Pythagoras-discovers-the-ratios plates, Boethius and the personified quadrivium, Robert Fludd's Temple of Music and musica mundana diagram, Kepler's planetary harmonies, Kircher's Musurgia Universalis, the Macrobian cosmos of the Dream of Scipio, the tetractys, and the monochord by which one string measures the whole tuned universe.

title: Kircher's Universe — The Baroque Machine of Universal Knowledge
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The encyclopedic engravings of the Jesuit polymath Athanasius Kircher (1602–1680), who tried to compress all of creation into a single Baroque 'machine of knowledge.' Spanning the central fire and underground oceans of the Mundus Subterraneus, the optics and sundials of the Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae, the cosmic organ of the Musurgia Universalis, the magnetic philosophy of the Magnes, the missionary marvels of China Illustrata, the Tower of Babel of the Turris Babel, and Noah's Ark — the visual encyclopedia of an esoteric encyclopedist whose erudition and credulity were equally vast.

title: Prophecy & Apocalyptic Figurae: Diagrams of Sacred History
creator: various
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: The visual culture of Western prophecy: Joachim of Fiore's apocalyptic *figurae* (the seven-headed dragon and trinitarian diagrams of the three ages), the *Vaticinia de Pontificibus* papal-prophecy emblems, the enigmatic *Prophéties* of Nostradamus, Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio*, the prophesying sibyls of antiquity reborn in Renaissance art, the Tiburtine Sibyl's vision before Augustus, and the monstrous prodigies and comets read as omens of the end on Reformation broadsheets.

title: The Temple of Solomon & Sacred Architecture as a Model of the Cosmos
creator: various
place: Spain, Low Countries, France, German lands, England
rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
blurb: Prints and engravings in which the Temple of Solomon, the desert Tabernacle, and Ezekiel's visionary temple are reconstructed as scale models of the cosmos. Includes the Villalpando-tradition elevation, Callot, Fischer von Erlach and Dapper temple plates, the camp of Israel, the molten sea and twelve oxen, the two pillars Jachin and Boaz, the cherubim-guarded Ark, and the high priest's jeweled breastplate (Hoshen) as a star-map of the twelve tribes.
